findings.title: Sensitive directory metadata inspection in diagnostics
findings.description: The skill inspects the existence and permission modes of sensitive local directories such as ~/.ssh, ~/.aws, ~/.config, and ~/.gnupg. While it does not read file contents or exfiltrate data, it does enumerate security-relevant local paths and expose their metadata in output. In a broader agent workflow, this could reveal environmental details beyond the minimum needed for generic PATH troubleshooting.
findings.remediation: Limit diagnostics to directories directly relevant to the reported issue, require user confirmation before inspecting sensitive paths, and clearly disclose what metadata will be collected.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The code does enumerate and print metadata for security-sensitive directories (`~/.ssh`, `~/.aws`, `~/.config`, `~/.gnupg`), which can disclose environmental details, though it does not read contents or exfiltrate data.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Low; an attacker would need access to the diagnostic output or an agent workflow that exposes it.
metadata.meta_impact: Limited disclosure of local environment structure and permission modes for sensitive directories.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill appears generally benign and focused on local PATH/filesystem diagnostics. No prompt injection, exfiltration, obfuscation, command injection, or external network activity was identified. The main concerns are minor: inspection of sensitive directory metadata and missing manifest restrictions that would improve transparency.

meta_correlations.relationship: All three findings flag normal diagnostic command execution, but the actual code uses fixed commands only: `find ... -exec test -e {}` merely checks symlink targets, and `subprocess.run([tool] + flag.split(), ...)` invokes predefined version commands without user-controlled input.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: No security fix required. If desired, reduce scanner noise by adding comments that these commands are fixed diagnostic checks and keep subprocess arguments as explicit lists rather than shell strings.

meta_correlations.relationship: Both findings concern omitted manifest metadata rather than an exploitable security flaw.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Add a proper YAML front matter manifest with `license`, `compatibility`, and optionally `allowed-tools` to improve transparency and reduce policy noise.

meta_recommendations.title: Gate sensitive-directory checks behind explicit user consent
meta_recommendations.fix: Change `check_sensitive_permissions()` to run only when the user asks for permission auditing, or restrict output to a generic warning such as `sensitive directory permissions need review` without printing exact paths and modes by default.

meta_recommendations.title: Add explicit manifest metadata
meta_recommendations.fix:
Add YAML front matter to `SKILL.md`, for example: `---
name: path-doctor
description: Diagnose and fix PATH and filesystem issues
license: MIT
compatibility: [Claude.ai, Claude Code]
allowed-tools: [Read, Bash, Python]
---`

meta_recommendations.title: Document fixed-command safety for subprocess and find usage
meta_recommendations.fix: Keep command arguments hardcoded as lists, avoid `shell=True`, and add inline comments such as `# fixed diagnostic command, no user input` near `subprocess.run(...)` and the `find ... -exec test -e {}` example.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: This skill is a benign local diagnostics utility; the only real security concern is low-risk disclosure of local directory permission metadata, while the command-injection and manifest findings are false positives or informational noise.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Minimize or gate reporting of sensitive-directory metadata such as ~/.ssh and ~/.aws unless explicitly requested by the user.
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: The code performs local PATH and permission diagnostics only, with no network access, no tainted command construction, no file-content collection, and no malicious or deceptive behavior.
